Implications for Public Health Practice 
To protect against measles and its complications before traveling internationally, all persons aged ≥12 months should have documented receipt of 2 appropriately spaced doses of MMR vaccine, and infants aged 6–11 months of age should receive 1 dose of MMR vaccine ( 10 ). Persons residing in or traveling domestically to outbreak areas should follow local public health guidance, which is developed based on review and analysis of the local outbreak epidemiology ( 6 ). Infants aged <6 months are at high risk for measles complications but are too young to be vaccinated, and therefore depend upon population immunity and passively transferred maternal measles antibodies (from previously vaccinated or infected mothers) to prevent infections and related complications.
Health care providers continue to serve on the front lines to identify measles cases, alert public health departments ¶¶¶ , ensure recommended testing, and implement measles isolation precautions to prevent health care–associated and community-based transmission.
